Second, co-evolutionary analysis (mirrortree) and protein-protein interaction network prediction (STRING, structural modeling with AlphaFold2) will identify probable host interaction partners. Third, predicted functions will be validated experimentally using co-immunoprecipitation and apoptosis assays in MCV-positive cell lines to test interactions with caspase-8 and other DED-containing host proteins.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic functional assignment for the unique MCV gene families, directly testing their role in apoptosis modulation. It will elucidate novel viral strategies for host subversion and offer insights into the molecular evolution of polyomaviruses, potentially revealing new therapeutic targets for MCV-associated cancers.
proposals.novelty: While MCV's unique genomic structure is known, the specific molecular functions and host targets of its distinctive gene families are entirely unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates Koonin's expertise in protein family evolution and 'conserved hypothetical' protein analysis to de-orphanize these viral genes, moving beyond sequence annotation to testable mechanistic hypotheses.

proposals.title: Evolutionary and Functional Analysis of Conserved Cysteine Patterns in Uncharacterized NTPase and β-CASP Protein Families
proposals.motivation: A vast number of protein families, especially in archaea and bacteria, remain functionally uncharacterized despite containing conserved cysteine residues and putative redox-active motifs. These 'conserved hypothetical' proteins, including many NTPases and β-CASP nucleases, may represent novel redox sensors, regulators, or enzymes critical for cellular adaptation and genome maintenance, but their specific biochemical roles and evolutionary trajectories are unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic analysis of conserved cysteine patterns and co-evolving residues in these families will reveal distinct, evolutionarily stable functional modules (e.g., redox-sensitive switches, metal-coordinating centers) that dictate protein activity and interaction networks, particularly in relation to template (nucleic acid) metabolism.
proposals.method: We will employ sensitive sequence profile analysis (HHpred, PSI-BLAST) and comparative genomics to identify protein families with invariant cysteine patterns. For these families, we will predict 3D structures using AlphaFold2 and RoseTTAFold, focusing on the spatial clustering of cysteines and potential redox centers. Co-evolution analysis (using EVcouplings and direct coupling analysis) will be used to infer residues functionally linked to the cysteines. Predicted redox-active or metal-binding families will be prioritized for experimental validation of ATPase/nuclease activity under varying redox conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a functional classification for numerous uncharacterized protein families, linking specific cysteine architectures to potential redox biology in nucleic acid processing. It will generate testable mechanistic models and a prioritized list of high-impact targets for structural and biochemical studies, moving beyond sequence-based annotation to structure-function-evolution integration.
proposals.novelty: While cysteine conservation is noted, a systematic evolutionary-structural analysis targeting uncharacterized NTPase and β-CASP families to decipher the 'redox code' underlying their potential division of labor in template metabolism has not been conducted. The integration of co-evolutionary signals with deep learning-based structural predictions to hypothesize specific biochemical mechanisms is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Poxvirus Macroevolution: A Phylogenomic Analysis of Conserved Core Genes and the Diversification of NTPase Families
proposals.motivation: Poxviruses are large DNA viruses with significant impacts on human and animal health, yet their deep evolutionary history remains contentious, particularly regarding their origins and the timing of major host transitions. Pre-2026 sequencing efforts have dramatically increased the number of available poxvirus genomes, but comprehensive phylogenomic analyses incorporating these new data are lacking. Resolving this phylogeny is critical for understanding the evolution of viral pathogenesis, host range, and core replication machinery, including key NTPase proteins involved in transcription and DNA replication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a phylogenomic analysis incorporating all newly sequenced poxvirus genomes will reveal a more resolved and robust evolutionary tree, clarifying the relationships between major subfamilies (Chordopoxvirinae, Entomopoxvirinae) and identifying conserved, lineage-specific patterns of gene gain and loss, particularly among NTPase-encoding genes.
proposals.method: We will compile a comprehensive dataset of all publicly available (pre-2026) poxvirus genomes. A core set of single-copy orthologous genes will be identified using OrthoMCL or a similar clustering algorithm. Multiple sequence alignments for each core gene will be constructed using MAFFT, followed by concatenation. Maximum-likelihood phylogenies will be inferred using IQ-TREE 2 with model testing, and Bayesian dating will be performed using BEAST2 with carefully chosen fossil calibrations (e.g., historical variola sequences). Parallel analysis will focus on the evolution of NTPase gene families (e.g., D5-like, D6R-like helicases) using custom HMM profiles and gene tree-species tree reconciliation methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the most detailed and data-rich phylogenetic framework for Poxviridae to date, serving as a reference for future evolutionary studies. It will directly test competing hypotheses about poxvirus origins and the direction of host jumps. Furthermore, by mapping the evolution of NTPase proteins—key functional determinants—onto a robust tree, we will generate specific, testable predictions about the function of uncharacterized viral proteins in different lineages.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies used limited genomic samples, this analysis leverages the full genomic diversity available pre-2026 to perform a rigorous, dating-aware macroevolutionary reconstruction. The integrated focus on the evolution of NTPase protein families within a clarified phylogenetic context directly connects Koonin's long-standing interests in protein function and virus evolution to a concrete, high-impact viral system.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput Yeast Two-Hybrid Protein Interaction Map for Merkel Cell Polyomavirus: Identifying Functional Modules and Conserved Hypothetical Protein Partners
proposals.motivation: Merkel Cell Polyomavirus (MCV) is a major cause of Merkel cell carcinoma, but the functions of many of its proteins, especially the unique and conserved hypothetical ones, remain poorly characterized. Systematic mapping of viral protein-protein interactions (PPIs) is a proven strategy for inferring protein function and revealing viral replication and oncogenic mechanisms. A comprehensive MCV interactome is lacking and would directly address the challenge of prioritizing 'conserved hypothetical' proteins for experimental study, a key theme in Koonin's prior work.
proposals.hypothesis: High-throughput yeast two-hybrid screening will reveal a dense network of specific interactions among MCV proteins, identifying novel functional modules and assigning putative roles to currently uncharacterized viral proteins through their association with known functional partners.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput, matrix-based yeast two-hybrid (Y2H) system. All MCV open reading frames (including Large T, small T, VP1, VP2, VP3, and the unique MCV proteins) will be cloned into both GAL4 DNA-binding domain (bait) and activation domain (prey) vectors. A comprehensive all-by-all pairwise screen will be conducted in yeast strain AH109, with interactions scored by growth on selective media (-Leu/-Trp/-His/-Ade) and β-galactosidase assays. Positive interactions will be validated by retransformation and co-immunoprecipitation in mammalian cells. The resulting network will be analyzed using graph theory to identify highly connected hubs and functional modules.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic protein interaction map for MCV, providing a functional context for its unique and conserved hypothetical proteins. It will generate specific, testable hypotheses about viral replication complexes, pathogenesis, and the roles of uncharacterized viral NTPases and other proteins, directly informing future mechanistic studies in virology and oncology.
proposals.novelty: While Y2H has been used for other viruses, a complete, all-by-all interaction screen for MCV has not been performed. The focus on linking MCV's unique genomic repertoire, including predicted NTPases, to core viral functions through interaction mapping represents a novel integration of Koonin's themes of protein function prediction and template-catalyst systems into an oncogenic viral context.

proposals.title: Defining the Minimal Replicon of Merkel Cell Polyomavirus: A Systematic Deletion and Complementation Analysis
proposals.motivation: Merkel Cell Polyomavirus (MCV) is the established cause of Merkel cell carcinoma, but the fundamental logic of its small genome remains poorly understood. Defining the minimal set of viral genes required for autonomous replication in a permissive cell line would reveal the core, non-redundant functional modules and distinguish essential viral functions from those that are host-complemented or auxiliary. This aligns with Koonin's long-standing interest in the evolution of minimal replicators and the division of labor between genetic templates and catalytic functions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the minimal MCV replicon comprises a core subset of early and late genes encoding origin-binding and helicase functions (Large T antigen domains), alongside essential structural components for packaging, but is dispensable for several 'accessory' genes currently annotated in the viral genome.
proposals.method: We will employ a systematic, PCR-based deletion mutagenesis approach on an infectious MCV clone to generate a comprehensive library of single- and multi-gene knockout constructs. Replication competence will be assayed by quantitative PCR for viral DNA replication in permissive MKL-1 cells. Essential genes identified will be validated via *trans*-complementation assays with expression plasmids. Bioinformatic analysis, leveraging Koonin's expertise in protein family (e.g., SF3 helicase/ATPase) and 'conserved hypothetical' protein prioritization, will characterize the predicted functions of essential genes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first functional map of MCV genetic essentiality, identifying the virus's true minimal genome. It will provide a framework for understanding polyomavirus persistence and pathogenesis by distinguishing core viral replicative machinery from host-dependent processes. The results will also offer novel targets for antiviral strategies aimed at disrupting essential viral replication.
proposals.novelty: While MCV genes have been studied individually, a systematic, whole-genome deletion analysis to define a minimal replicon has not been performed. This approach moves beyond correlative studies to a definitive functional catalog, applying principles of minimal genome analysis used in cellular systems to a small, oncogenic DNA virus.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating NTPase Superfamily Boundaries: A Multi-Domain and Phylogenetic Analysis to Challenge Overly Broad Motif-Based Groupings
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, protein superfamilies are often defined by the presence of a conserved short sequence motif, such as the Walker A/P-loop in NTPases, implying a common evolutionary origin and mechanistic core. However, this practice risks grouping together proteins with distinct domain architectures, evolutionary histories, and biochemical functions, leading to misleading functional predictions and obscuring true phylogenetic relationships. This is particularly problematic for prioritizing 'conserved hypothetical' proteins for experimental study, as their annotation is often based solely on such broad motif signatures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current superfamily delineations for major NTPase clans (e.g., P-loop NTPases) are overly broad and contain distinct, evolutionarily independent lineages that converged on a similar phosphate-binding motif. These lineages can be distinguished through integrated analysis of full-length sequence, domain architecture, and phylogenetic context.
proposals.method: We will perform a large-scale computational analysis focusing on P-loop NTPases. Using sensitive profile hidden Markov models (HMMs) from Pfam and the CDD, we will identify all proteins containing the P-loop motif across sequenced genomes. We will then cluster these sequences using deep, iterative homology searches (HHblits) and construct multiple sequence alignments for each cluster. Phylogenetic trees will be built using maximum likelihood methods (e.g., IQ-TREE). Crucially, we will integrate analysis of full-domain architectures (via RPS-BLAST against the CDD) and structural predictions (using AlphaFold2, as available pre-2026) to identify clades defined by consistent, unique domain fusions and structural features beyond the core motif.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more accurate, phylogenetically grounded map of NTPase diversity, potentially leading to the subdivision of overly broad superfamilies into evolutionarily coherent families. It will generate specific, testable predictions about the functional divergence of newly delineated groups, directly aiding the experimental prioritization of uncharacterized members. The findings will challenge the paradigm of single-motif-based classification and offer a revised framework for understanding the evolution of template-processing enzymes central to replication and translation.
proposals.novelty: While motif-based superfamily definitions are widely accepted, systematically challenging their validity by integrating full-domain architecture and deep phylogenetics to reveal convergent evolution within these groups is not routine. This approach moves beyond cataloging motifs to reconstructing evolutionary trajectories, testing the fundamental assumption of common descent underlying superfamily classification.

proposals.title: A Pan-Domain Census and Evolutionary Analysis of NTP-Binding Protein Families: Tracing the Deep History of Molecular Replication and Cellular Energetics
proposals.motivation: NTPases are fundamental to life, powering replication, translation, signaling, and transport. While individual families are well-studied, a systematic, domain-wide comparative analysis across all three domains of life (Archaea, Bacteria, Eukaryota) is lacking. Such a census is crucial for understanding the early evolution of the central cellular machinery, particularly the division of labor between template and catalyst, a key theme in the origin of genomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a core set of NTPase folds and families will be universally conserved across all domains of life, representing an ancient, pre-LUCA functional repertoire, while domain-specific expansions will reveal key innovations linked to the diversification of archaeal, bacterial, and eukaryotic cellular architectures.
proposals.method: We will apply sensitive sequence profile (HMMER) and structure prediction (AlphaFold2, pre-2026 version) searches to major databases (UniRef, NCBI) to identify all NTP-binding proteins in representative proteomes. Proteins will be clustered into families using MMseqs2 and classified by fold (using CATH/ECOD). Phylogenetic analysis will be performed for universal families using maximum likelihood (IQ-TREE), and gene gain/loss events will be mapped onto a species tree using Count. Functional annotation will integrate genomic context and predicted structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first definitive catalog of NTPase families across the tree of life, distinguishing universal from lineage-specific components. It will provide a concrete evolutionary framework for experimental studies of 'conserved hypothetical' NTPases and test models for the early co-evolution of replication and metabolic modules.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, three-domain scale of the analysis, moving beyond studies of single families or two domains, and in explicitly linking the distribution of NTPase families to major transitions in cellular evolution and the origin of DNA genomes.

proposals.title: Viral Replication Machinery Homologs: A Systematic Phylogenomic Search for the Origins of Viral DNA Replication and the Division of Labor
proposals.motivation: The evolutionary origins of viral replication machinery, particularly for DNA viruses, remain enigmatic. Pre-2026 research, including Koonin's work on the division of labor between template and catalyst, suggests that core replication proteins like DNA polymerases and helicases may have deep evolutionary roots, potentially predating the last universal cellular ancestor (LUCA) or being recruited from cellular lineages. A systematic search for homologs of these proteins across the global virome is crucial to map these origins and test competing hypotheses of viral genome evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: Core viral replication proteins, especially those with NTPase domains (e.g., helicases, primases) and specific DNA polymerases, possess ancient homologs not only in cellular organisms but also across diverse, understudied viral groups, revealing a complex network of horizontal gene transfer and ancient divergence that traces back to the early evolution of replicators.
proposals.method: We will perform an exhaustive, iterative homology search using HMMER3 and PSI-BLAST against comprehensive viral and cellular protein databases (NCBI RefSeq, metagenomic viral contigs). Starting seeds will include experimentally characterized replication proteins from major viral families and archaeal systems. Predicted protein domains (via CDD, Pfam) and structural predictions (using AlphaFold2, pre-2026 version) will guide the validation of distant homologs. Phylogenetic analysis of identified families will be conducted using maximum-likelihood (IQ-TREE) and Bayesian methods to infer deep evolutionary relationships.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a detailed map of the evolutionary connections between viral and cellular replication machineries, identifying new 'conserved hypothetical' viral proteins as high-priority targets for functional characterization. It will directly test hypotheses on the antiquity of specific replication protein folds and provide a robust dataset for understanding the transition from RNA to DNA genomes in the virosphere.
proposals.novelty: Unlike previous focused studies on specific viral families, this proposal systematically targets the entire diversity of viral genomes, including metagenomic data, to search for deeply conserved homologs, explicitly integrating the concepts of template-catalyst division of labor and the functional prediction of uncharacterized proteins into viral evolution.

proposals.title: Structural Homology of NTPase Domains in Replication-Associated Proteins: Uncovering Deep Evolutionary Links between Template and Catalyst
proposals.motivation: The origin of replication systems required the co-evolution of template and catalytic functions, with NTPases playing a central role in modern replication, transcription, and RNA processing machineries. Sequence-based homology detection often fails for these ancient protein families, leaving deep evolutionary relationships and the functional diversification of key 'conserved hypothetical' proteins unresolved. This limits our understanding of the fundamental division of labor in early replicator systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Advanced protein structure comparison will reveal distant homology between NTPase domains in diverse replication-associated proteins, including those from model replicator systems, archaeal β-CASP proteins, and other 'conserved hypothetical' targets, uncovering a shared structural and functional core predating the last universal common ancestor.
proposals.method: We will apply state-of-the-art, pre-2026 structural alignment algorithms (e.g., Dali, TM-align, and CE) to a curated dataset of protein structures. The dataset will include known replication-associated NTPases (e.g., helicases, polymerases), the novel archaeal β-CASP protein with KH domains, and high-priority 'conserved hypothetical' proteins predicted to have NTPase or nucleic acid-binding functions. The analysis will focus on detecting conserved structural folds and spatial arrangements of functional motifs beyond the reach of sequence-based methods like PSI-BLAST.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a structural-evolutionary framework linking disparate protein families involved in template-directed processes, directly testing models on the origin of DNA genomes. It will generate specific, testable functional predictions for prioritized 'conserved hypothetical' proteins, guiding experimental studies. The findings will refine the classification of NTPase families and their role in the early evolution of replication.
proposals.novelty: While structure comparison is established, its systematic application to bridge the conceptual gap between theoretical models of early replicators (division of labor) and concrete, extant protein families (especially archaeal and hypothetical ones) is novel. This integrates Koonin's themes by using structural data to trace the evolution of function from a pre-LUCA NTPase toolkit.

proposals.title: Deciphering Functional Interfaces in Divergent NTPase Families through Co-evolutionary Analysis and Contact Prediction
proposals.motivation: A vast number of protein families, especially those involved in replication and translation, contain conserved but highly divergent members whose specific molecular interactions remain unknown. This includes many 'conserved hypothetical' proteins and families of NTPases, which are central to template-dependent processes. Pre-2026, residue co-evolution analysis has successfully predicted 3D contacts in single protein families, but its systematic application to define functional interfaces across divergent subfamilies within a larger superfamily is underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Residue co-evolution patterns, analyzed across divergent subfamilies of a widespread NTPase superfamily (e.g., P-loop NTPases), will reveal conserved, subfamily-specific networks of contacting residues that correspond to functional interfaces for template, ligand, or partner protein binding.
proposals.method: We will select a large, diverse superfamily of NTPases (e.g., from the COG database) and define its major subfamilies using phylogenomics. For each subfamily, we will build deep multiple sequence alignments and apply direct coupling analysis (DCA) using the EVcouplings framework (pre-2026 state-of-the-art) to predict residue-residue contacts. We will compare contact maps across subfamilies to identify conserved core contacts versus subfamily-specific interface networks. These predicted interfaces will be mapped onto available 3D structures and validated through targeted mutagenesis in model archaeal systems, focusing on proteins with KH or other RNA-binding domains linked to NTPase activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computational and experimental framework for moving from general family annotation to specific interface prediction for divergent proteins. It will generate testable models for the molecular functions of uncharacterized NTPases, directly addressing Koonin's prior work on prioritizing 'conserved hypothetical' proteins. The results will offer insights into the evolution of functional specialization within ancient protein folds central to information processing.
proposals.novelty: While co-evolution is used for single-family structure prediction, its systematic application to dissect functional divergence *between* subfamilies of a superfamily, specifically to pinpoint variable interaction interfaces, is a novel synthesis of phylogenomics and contact prediction. This approach directly bridges evolutionary analysis and molecular mechanism in the context of Koonin's long-standing interest in the evolution of template-dependent processes.

proposals.title: Systematic Homology Network Analysis of NTPase Domains Across the Mobilome Reveals Deep Evolutionary Connections and Functional Diversification
proposals.motivation: The evolutionary history of key protein domains, particularly NTPases involved in replication and genome maintenance, is often studied within isolated viral lineages. However, the full mobilome—encompassing viruses, plasmids, transposons, and other mobile genetic elements (MGEs)—constitutes a vast, interconnected network of genetic exchange. A comprehensive, cross-mobilome analysis is lacking but is critical for understanding the deep evolutionary origins and horizontal transfer of fundamental molecular machinery that shapes genome architecture and cellular function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic homology searches will reveal a shared, ancient core of NTPase domains (e.g., P-loop NTPases, AAA+ ATPases, helicases) distributed across all major classes of MGEs, forming an evolutionary network that traces the flow of replicative and maintenance functions beyond the virosphere into the broader genetic mobilome.
proposals.method: We will scale up homology detection using iterative profile-profile comparisons with HHpred and PSI-BLAST, starting from curated seed alignments of viral NTPase families (e.g., from Koonin's previous work). This analysis will be expanded to comprehensive, pre-2026 protein sequence databases from all MGE types, including plasmids, integrative conjugative elements, and transposons from archaea, bacteria, and eukaryotes. Predicted protein domains will be annotated, and a network of significant sequence similarities (E-value < 1e-5) will be constructed and analyzed to identify central hub families and potential evolutionary pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first integrated map of NTPase domain distribution across the entire mobilome, identifying 'conserved hypothetical' proteins in MGEs as potential key players in genome dynamics. It will directly test the 'division of labor' model between template and catalyst in a broader evolutionary context and provide a prioritized list of MGE-encoded NTPase families for future experimental characterization of their biochemical activities.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have focused on viruses or specific cellular lineages, the novelty lies in the systematic, network-based integration of all mobile genetic elements, explicitly testing for deep evolutionary connections that transcend traditional MGE classification boundaries and may point to a common ancestral pool of replicative modules.

proposals.title: Mapping the ssDNA and Replication Intermediate Binding Interfaces of the ImuA-ImuB Translesion DNA Polymerase Accessory Complex
proposals.motivation: The ImuA-ImuB-ImuC (DNA Pol V) complex is a cornerstone of the mutagenic DNA damage tolerance system in bacteria lacking UmuDC, particularly in pathogens like Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Pre-2026 studies have established ImuB as a predicted NTPase and ImuA as a scaffold, but the precise molecular interfaces for binding single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) and replication fork intermediates remain unknown, hindering a mechanistic understanding of translesion synthesis recruitment and regulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ImuA and ImuB contain distinct, conserved, and cooperative binding surfaces for ssDNA and replication fork structures, likely involving their predicted NTPase and protein-protein interaction domains, which are essential for the complex's localization and function at stalled replication forks.
proposals.method: We will employ an integrated computational and experimental pipeline. First, we will use co-evolutionary analysis (direct coupling analysis) and structure prediction (AlphaFold2 multimer) to model the ImuA-ImuB complex and identify putative DNA-binding regions. These predictions will guide site-directed mutagenesis of candidate residues in Mycobacterium smegmatis ImuA/B. Binding interfaces will be experimentally validated using a combination of electrophoretic mobility shift assays with defined DNA substrates (ssDNA, gapped DNA, and fork-like structures) and targeted cross-linking mass spectrometry (XL-MS) to capture protein-DNA contacts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first residue-resolution map of DNA binding by the ImuA-ImuB complex, defining the molecular basis for its recruitment to damage sites. It will clarify the division of labor between template handling (ImuA/B) and catalysis (ImuC), a key evolutionary step in replicase systems, and identify potential targets for inhibiting mutagenesis in pathogenic mycobacteria.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identified ImuA/B as essential, the specific amino acids responsible for DNA substrate recognition are entirely unknown. This proposal moves beyond genetic necessity to mechanistic, structural definition, integrating state-of-the-art pre-2026 predictive methods with focused biochemistry to deconstruct a 'conserved hypothetical' functional module.

proposals.title: Polyphyletic Origins of Viral Supergroups: Testing the Independent Acquisition of Conserved NTPase Domains in Nucleocytoplasmic Large DNA Viruses
proposals.motivation: Viral 'supergroups,' such as the proposed order Megavirales encompassing nucleocytoplasmic large DNA viruses (NCLDVs), are often defined by a core set of conserved genes, including key NTPases involved in replication and transcription. The assumption of monophyly for these groups underpins models of viral macroevolution. However, the possibility that these conserved domains were acquired independently via horizontal gene transfer (HGT) from cellular hosts or other viruses in different lineages challenges this fundamental premise and requires rigorous testing.
proposals.hypothesis: The conserved NTPase domains defining major NCLDV lineages (e.g., in the A32-like packaging ATPase or transcription machinery components) are not homologous by common viral descent but are the result of multiple, independent acquisitions from cellular or viral sources, rendering the proposed viral supergroup polyphyletic.
proposals.method: We will perform an exhaustive comparative genomic analysis focusing on the identified NTPase families. Using sensitive profile hidden Markov model searches (HMMER) against viral and cellular sequence databases, we will compile comprehensive sequence sets. Phylogenetic trees of these NTPases will be reconstructed using maximum likelihood (IQ-TREE) and Bayesian methods (MrBayes). We will then critically compare these gene trees to a robust, consensus virus phylogeny based on concatenated capsid and DNA polymerase proteins, searching for strongly supported, conflicting topologies indicative of HGT. Network analysis (SplitsTree) will be used to visualize conflicting signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, data-driven test of the monophyly of a major viral assemblage, directly addressing a core assumption in virology. It will clarify the evolutionary mechanisms—vertical descent versus rampant HGT—shaping the genomes of giant viruses. The findings will inform the prioritization of truly conserved 'hallmark genes' for phylogenetic classification versus those prone to horizontal exchange.
proposals.novelty: While HGT is acknowledged in virus evolution, systematic testing of polyphyly for an entire proposed viral order defined by conserved NTPase functions is lacking. This approach moves beyond cataloging gene presence/absence to explicitly contrasting gene tree topologies with a species tree, a method common in cellular phylogenomics but not yet rigorously applied to challenge viral supergroup monophyly.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the functional role of invariant motifs in NTPase superfamilies: A combined evolutionary and structural analysis of conserved residues in 'hypothetical' proteins.
proposals.motivation: The functional annotation of conserved hypothetical proteins remains a major challenge in genomics. Many contain canonical motifs (e.g., Walker A/B, P-loop, His-Asp-Arg clusters) typically associated with catalysis in NTPases, yet their specific biochemical roles are unknown. This ambiguity hinders understanding of fundamental cellular processes and the evolution of protein families, a core interest of Koonin's lab. Determining whether extreme conservation signifies a catalytic or a structural role is critical for accurate functional prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a significant subset of conserved hypothetical proteins within NTPase superfamilies, invariant residues in classic catalytic motifs (e.g., His, AspXXArg, Tyr) are essential not for direct chemistry but for maintaining structural integrity or mediating critical protein-protein or protein-nucleic acid interactions necessary for template-dependent functions.
proposals.method: We will employ a phylogenomics pipeline to identify divergent clades within major NTPase families (e.g., AAA+, SF1/SF2 helicases) where canonical motifs are preserved but contextual sequence signatures suggest functional divergence. For selected targets, we will use AlphaFold2 (pre-2026 version) and trRosetta to predict high-confidence structural models, focusing on the spatial organization of invariant motifs. Molecular dynamics simulations (using GROMACS) will assess the structural and dynamic consequences of in silico mutations at these sites. Predicted interaction interfaces will be tested experimentally via co-purification assays with candidate partners (e.g., tRNA, ssDNA).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for distinguishing between catalytic and structural conservation in enzyme superfamilies, directly addressing the prioritization problem for experimental study. It will generate specific, testable models for the function of numerous 'hypothetical' proteins, potentially revealing novel mechanisms of template recognition and processing in nucleic acid metabolism.
proposals.novelty: While motifs like Walker A are universally linked to NTP hydrolysis, systematically testing the null hypothesis—that their conservation primarily serves a structural scaffold role in specific lineages—is non-obvious. This shifts the focus from assumed chemistry to the evolution of structural networks supporting the division of labor between template and catalyst.

proposals.title: Structural Elucidation of a Conserved Viral NTPase Domain: Implications for Template-Dependent Replication Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: A significant fraction of proteins encoded in viral and cellular genomes, particularly those of archaeal viruses and giant viruses, remain functionally uncharacterized despite being predicted to contain NTPase domains. These domains are frequently implicated in nucleic acid metabolism and replication, a core theme in Koonin's work on the origin of genomes and the division of labor between template and catalyst. Determining the structure of such a conserved yet uncharacterized domain is a critical step towards understanding its specific biochemical function in viral replication cycles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the three-dimensional structure of this viral NTPase domain, solved by X-ray crystallography, will reveal a unique fold or domain arrangement that suggests a specific template-binding (e.g., DNA/RNA) and catalytic mechanism, distinguishing it from other known NTPase families.
proposals.method: The target viral protein gene will be cloned and expressed in E. coli. The protein will be purified via affinity and size-exclusion chromatography. Crystallization trials will be performed using high-throughput robotic screening of commercial sparse-matrix screens. Optimized crystals will be used to collect X-ray diffraction data at a synchrotron source. The phase problem will be solved by molecular replacement using predicted AlphaFold2 models or, if necessary, by experimental phasing (e.g., selenomethionine MAD). The structure will be refined using Phenix and Coot.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution structural data for a phylogenetically widespread but functionally obscure viral protein family. It will directly inform mechanistic models of viral genome replication and offer a structural template for interpreting genomic context and homology, thereby prioritizing this protein for detailed biochemical study of its NTPase and potential helicase/nuclease activities.
proposals.novelty: While many NTPase structures are known, this proposal targets a specific, conserved 'hypothetical' protein family from viruses that has been predicted but never experimentally characterized, bridging genomic prediction with concrete structural biology to test evolutionary hypotheses about replication protein diversification.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Footprinting of Subtle Conserved Motifs in NTPase and Template-Processing Protein Families
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, a significant fraction of protein families, especially those involved in fundamental processes like replication and translation (e.g., NTPases, template-processing proteins), contain 'conserved hypothetical' members with unclear active sites or functional surfaces. Standard sequence alignment often fails to detect subtle, low-entropy residues critical for function, particularly in large, diverse families. This gap hinders the functional annotation of these proteins, a long-standing interest in Koonin's work on genome evolution and protein function prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying sensitive phylogenetic footprinting algorithms to deeply sampled, structurally aligned protein families will reveal previously undetected, weakly conserved sequence motifs that correspond to functional surfaces or regulatory sites, beyond the canonical catalytic cores.
proposals.method: We will select protein families central to Koonin's themes (e.g., specific NTPase clades, β-CASP nucleases, or replication-associated proteins). For each, we will construct a comprehensive, high-quality multiple sequence alignment using PROMALS3D, integrating structural data where available. We will then apply the phylogenetic footprinting algorithm implemented in the FootPrinter tool suite, using maximum parsimony to identify conserved positions across evolutionary sub-trees. This will be complemented by entropy-based analysis (like Rate4Site) to quantify conservation strength. Predicted motifs will be mapped onto known 3D structures (e.g., from the cited β-CASP protein study) for spatial validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate specific, testable predictions for functional residues in under-characterized members of key protein families, directly aiding the prioritization of 'conserved hypothetical' proteins for experimental study. It will provide a methodological framework for moving beyond catalytic residue identification to uncover regulatory or interaction motifs, deepening our understanding of the division of labor in molecular systems.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic footprinting is established in genomics, its systematic application to detect *subtle*, non-catalytic motifs within diverse, functionally important protein domain families—integrating structural alignments and deep phylogenetic sampling—remains underexplored, especially for template-processing and NTPase enzymes central to early genome evolution.

proposals.title: Yeast Two-Hybrid Screening to Identify Host Protein Interactors of a Conserved Viral NTPase Domain: Elucidating Functional Context in Viral Replication
proposals.motivation: A significant fraction of viral proteomes, especially in large DNA viruses, consists of 'conserved hypothetical' proteins, many predicted to contain NTPase domains. These domains likely play crucial roles in viral genome replication and transcription, but their specific molecular functions and host interaction partners remain unknown. Identifying these interactors is essential for understanding the division of labor between viral templates and catalysts, a core theme in the evolution of replicator systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The conserved viral NTPase domain of interest directly interacts with specific host nuclear proteins involved in nucleic acid metabolism, and these interactions are critical for the domain's function in viral genome replication or transcription.
proposals.method: We will employ a comprehensive yeast two-hybrid (Y2H) screen. The viral NTPase domain will be cloned as the 'bait' in a DNA-binding domain vector. A high-complexity human cDNA library, pre-dating 2026, will serve as the 'prey'. The screen will be performed using standard auxotrophic selection on media lacking histidine and adenine, with β-galactosidase assays for validation. Primary hits will be confirmed by retransformation and co-immunoprecipitation in mammalian cells. Interacting proteins will be analyzed using pre-2026 databases (e.g., STRING, Gene Ontology) to identify functional networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first functional context for a conserved viral NTPase domain by mapping its direct host protein interaction network. It will prioritize specific host pathways (e.g., DNA repair, transcription, RNA processing) for mechanistic follow-up, moving the protein from a 'conserved hypothetical' to a functionally annotated entity. The findings will offer insights into how viruses co-opt host machinery, informing models of virus-host co-evolution.
proposals.novelty: While Y2H is a standard method, its targeted application to a specific, uncharacterized viral NTPase domain—prioritized from genomic analyses of viral 'conserved hypothetical' proteins—is novel. The approach directly tests the hypothesis that such domains function through specific host partnerships, a non-obvious strategy given the typical focus on viral protein self-assembly.

proposals.title: Widespread Horizontal Transfer and Functional Diversification of a Viral RNA Methyltransferase Domain in Cellular Genomes
proposals.motivation: Viral proteins often have cellular homologs, revealing deep evolutionary connections and mechanisms of host-virus co-evolution. The discovery of a novel viral methyltransferase domain, distinct from known families, presents an opportunity to trace its evolutionary history. Identifying homologous sequences in cellular genomes could uncover previously unknown host pathways for RNA modification or regulation, and clarify the direction of transfer—whether from virus to host or host to virus.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sequences homologous to the novel viral methyltransferase domain are present in diverse eukaryotic and prokaryotic genomes, acquired via horizontal gene transfer, and that these cellular homologs have diversified in function beyond canonical RNA capping.
proposals.method: We will perform sensitive, iterative homology searches using PSI-BLAST and HMMER against the full NCBI non-redundant protein database, focusing on eukaryotic and prokaryotic lineages. Predicted protein domains will be analyzed using the CDD and Pfam. Structural homology will be assessed via HHpred. Positive hits will be subjected to phylogenetic reconstruction using maximum-likelihood methods (e.g., IQ-TREE) to infer evolutionary relationships and potential transfer events. Genomic context analysis will be performed to predict functional associations.
proposals.contribution: This work will expand the catalog of known RNA-modifying enzymes in cellular organisms and provide a concrete case study of virus-host gene exchange. It will either identify a new cellular RNA modification system or reveal a viral domestication event, contributing to our understanding of the functional repertoire of 'conserved hypothetical' proteins, a long-standing interest in the Koonin group.
proposals.novelty: While viral methyltransferases are known, the search for homologs of this specific, novel domain in cellular genomes is unexplored. The approach combines deep homology detection with evolutionary context analysis to move beyond simple cataloging toward inferring functional and evolutionary trajectories.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Divergence in a β-CASP Family Member: Biochemical and Structural Analysis of a Novel Archaeal N-Terminal Domain
proposals.motivation: The β-CASP family of nucleases within the metallo-β-lactamase superfamily is defined by a canonical chymotrypsin-like fold and a conserved catalytic mechanism. However, the discovery of archaeal β-CASP proteins with extensive, uncharacterized N-terminal domains (e.g., KH domains) suggests potential functional diversification beyond the core nuclease activity. Classifying these proteins solely by their C-terminal protease homology may obscure novel biochemical functions critical for understanding the evolution of nucleic acid metabolism in Archaea.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the N-terminal domain of the recently identified archaeal β-CASP protein (with KH domains) employs a catalytic mechanism distinct from the canonical chymotrypsin-like serine protease/hydrolase activity, potentially involving nucleotide binding or RNA interaction, despite sequence resemblance to protease folds.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational and experimental approach. First, we will use sensitive, structure-based homology detection tools (HHpred, Phyre2) and molecular dynamics simulations to analyze the N-terminal domain's predicted active site geometry and stability. Second, we will recombinantly express the full-length protein and isolated domains in E. coli. Catalytic activity will be tested using fluorogenic peptide substrates for protease activity and labeled oligonucleotides for nuclease/nucleotide-binding assays. Third, we will determine the high-resolution crystal structure of the N-terminal domain in apo and ligand-bound states to identify non-canonical active site residues and confirm mechanistic predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test and potentially redefine the functional classification of a 'conserved hypothetical' archaeal protein, moving it from a predicted protease to a mechanistically characterized factor. It will provide a framework for re-evaluating other β-CASP family members with complex domain architectures, contributing to a more nuanced understanding of the division of labor between template binding and catalysis in early evolutionary systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in challenging the automatic functional annotation based on domain homology and explicitly testing for mechanistic divergence within a well-established enzyme family, focusing on an archaeal system where such diversification is poorly explored but evolutionarily significant.

ideas.title: Systematically map the functional diversity of the A22R resolvase superfamily across viral and cellular genomes
ideas.description: This project integrates the functional virology of Idea A with the bioinformatic expansion of Idea B. We will first use sensitive sequence analysis and structural prediction on updated genomic databases to identify all divergent members of the A22R resolvase superfamily, including those in cellular organisms (e.g., bacteria, archaea) and other viruses. We will then select representative members from across this phylogenetic tree and test their in vivo activity using a vaccinia virus-based complementation assay, where the native A22R gene is replaced with these homologs. This approach will reveal which sequence motifs are essential for viral DNA recombination/replication and may uncover novel, non-canonical functions (e.g., in DNA repair or genome stability) performed by superfamily members in their native hosts.

ideas.title: Adapting Hidden Domain Detection from Virology to Find Divergent Metazoan Resolvases
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that metazoan cells lack bacterial-type resolvases by proposing they were missed due to sequence divergence. Drawing an analogy from virology, where highly divergent viral homologs of cellular enzymes were discovered using advanced bioinformatics, we adapt that solution. The analogous problem—finding functionally conserved enzymes with minimal sequence similarity—was solved using iterative profile-based searches (HHpred, PSI-BLAST) and fold recognition tools that prioritize structural conservation. Adapted to this context, the research idea involves constructing a sensitive computational pipeline using hidden Markov models seeded with a diverse set of RuvC/RNase H-like nucleases to scour metazoan proteomes. Candidates will be selected based on predicted conservation of the catalytic 'DEDD' motif and overall RNase H fold, not sequence percent identity. Top predicted hits would then be tested experimentally for Holliday junction binding and cleavage activity, potentially revealing a new class of metazoan genome maintenance enzymes.

ideas.description: This project proactively defines superfamily membership based on conserved structural and functional cores, moving beyond sequence similarity alone. It employs a pipeline that iteratively queries expanding genomic databases using sensitive structure comparison tools guided by probabilistic models of structural conservation. This enables the discovery of extremely divergent lineage-specific members and resolves ambiguous or polyphyletic groups that sequence-only methods misclassify. The result is a dynamic, principle-based classification that reveals deeper evolutionary relationships and functional innovations within protein superfamilies.

ideas.title: Adapting a Geochemical Prospecting Pipeline to Systematically Mine Diverse Viral Genomes for RuvC-like Domains
ideas.description: This idea adapts the systematic pipeline from geochemical mineral prospecting to the bioinformatic search for RuvC-like resolvases in large DNA viruses. In geochemistry, broad surveys identify anomalies for targeted follow-up. Adapted here, the first stage is a permissive computational 'survey' of diverse viral sequence space (including metagenomic assemblies from diverse ecosystems) to capture all potential RuvC-like domain homologs, even highly divergent ones. The second, high-resolution 'follow-up' stage uses advanced structural prediction and functional docking to rigorously validate and characterize the candidates from the first pass. This pipeline approach ensures a more comprehensive and efficient discovery of novel RuvC homologs across the viral universe than sequential, family-by-family screening.

ideas.title: Phylogenomic falsification of bacterial ancestry for viral RuvC via deep eukaryotic sampling and artifact-corrected models
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea seeks to definitively test the eukaryotic acquisition hypothesis by attempting to falsify the dominant bacterial-origin model. It employs a targeted sequencing effort to capture the full diversity of eukaryotic RuvC-like endonucleases, especially from under-sampled protist groups that may represent the ancestral eukaryotic state. The core analysis uses state-of-the-art phylogenomic methods designed to overcome systematic errors, explicitly modeling scenarios of horizontal gene transfer and measuring statistical support for a viral-eukaryotic clade exclusive of bacteria. The outcome is a robust, artifact-corrected phylogenetic framework that can either strongly support or reject the original premise.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Deep Mutational Scanning to Define Functional Hallmarks of Protein Superfamilies
ideas.description: This project integrates expanded superfamily sequence data with deep mutational scanning (DMS) on a minimal ancestral domain. First, newly identified divergent sequences from updated genomic databases are used to infer a more robust ancestral sequence and to define the full landscape of natural sequence variation. DMS is then performed on this ancestral domain to empirically map all functional constraints. By comparing the experimental DMS fitness landscape with the natural evolutionary variation, the study identifies 'functional hallmarks'—combinations of residues and interactions that are indispensable for function despite high sequence divergence. This unified approach allows for the functional annotation of remote homologs based on whether they preserve these core hallmarks, moving beyond simple sequence similarity.

ideas.title: Investigate eukaryotic-origin signaling nucleotidyltransferases in viral defense and host mimicry
ideas.description: This research direction hypothesizes that the newly identified family of bacterial/archaeal signal transduction nucleotidyltransferases (NTases) with ligand-binding domains has a deeper evolutionary link to eukaryotic signaling systems. By assuming viral RuvC homologs were acquired from an ancient eukaryotic host, we propose to search for viral NTase-RuvC fusion proteins or operons that may have co-opted eukaryotic ligand-sensing domains. The goal is to identify the eukaryotic signaling ligands these viral domains recognize and determine if they modulate nuclease activity to sense host cell state, such as stress or immune activation. This fusion investigates a potential ancient viral strategy of hijacking host signal transduction pathways to precisely time defensive or offensive activities like genome rearrangement or anti-host cleavage.

ideas.title: Functional and structural screening for RuvC-like DNA junction resolution activity in metazoan proteomes
ideas.description: This redesigned approach directly tests the core hypothesis by searching for the function, not just the sequence. It involves expressing metazoan protein libraries in E. coli strains deficient in RuvC or other Holliday junction resolvases and selecting for clones that restore resistance to DNA-damaging agents and proper chromosome segregation. Candidate proteins from positive screens are then purified and subjected to structural determination (e.g., cryo-EM or crystallography) to identify any that adopt the characteristic RuvC endonuclease fold. This method bypasses the limitation of sequence divergence by directly assaying for the conserved biochemical activity and three-dimensional architecture.

The goal is to identify peptide or small protein ligands that bind specifically to the AC catalytic pocket. By comparing the binding profiles and selected ligand sequences between ACs and distantly related polbeta superfamily members (e.g., DNA polymerases, nucleotidyltransferases), one can test if structural similarity correlates with convergent functional binding sites. This provides direct experimental evidence for or against the convergent evolution hypothesis, moving beyond purely bioinformatic analysis, and could uncover novel allosteric regulators or tools for ACs based on principles of structural mimicry.

ideas.title: Apply metagenomic assembly approaches to structural genomics for uncharacterized protein families
ideas.description: This approach adapts solutions from microbial metagenomics to structural genomics. In metagenomics, researchers sequence environmental DNA and use computational assembly to reconstruct complete genomes from fragmented sequences of uncultured organisms. Similarly, for uncharacterized protein families, we propose developing computational pipelines that integrate sparse structural data (partial electron density maps, low-resolution models, and fragment structures) from high-throughput crystallography of diverse family members. Using consensus modeling and fragment assembly algorithms inspired by metagenomic assemblers, we can reconstruct complete structural models for entire protein families from partial experimental data, dramatically increasing the efficiency of structural characterization compared to solving each structure independently.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Cartography: Mapping Superfamily Distribution with Astronomical Survey Techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodology of large-scale astronomical sky surveys to the problem of pan-genomic superfamily analysis. Just as surveys like the SDSS systematically map and classify celestial objects across vast volumes of space to reveal cosmic structure, this project will implement a standardized computational pipeline to survey the distribution and variants of a target protein superfamily across thousands of microbial genomes. The solution involves treating the collection of genomes as a structured landscape to be mapped. The pipeline will apply consistent 'filters' (e.g., HMM profiles, synteny checks) for detection and classification, generating a massive, searchable catalog of superfamily instances. The resulting distribution map will then be analyzed with techniques analogous to cosmological data analysis—using statistical measures of clustering, correlation functions, and network theory—to objectively refine phyletic patterns and distinguish vertical inheritance from horizontal gene transfer events based on distribution anomalies. This scale-driven, survey-based approach moves beyond individual genome comparisons to a population-level statistical understanding of superfamily evolution and dispersal.

ideas.title: Phylogenetically-grounded deep mutational scanning of ancestral domain ensembles
ideas.description: This approach first reconstructs an ensemble of plausible ancestral domain sequences, capturing the uncertainty inherent in phylogenetic inference. High-throughput deep mutational scanning is then performed on this entire ensemble of synthetic ancestral variants. By analyzing the resulting functional landscapes and comparing them to the historical pattern of amino acid substitutions observed in the descendant lineage, the method identifies which ancestral reconstructions produce functional constraints that best explain the actual evolutionary trajectory. This creates a feedback loop where functional data validates and refines the historical models, moving beyond a single speculative ancestor.

ideas.title: High-throughput structural phylogenomics to map functional divergence in uncharacterized protein superfamilies
ideas.description: This project integrates high-throughput X-ray crystallography pipelines with a structure-first functional discovery strategy. It targets large, sequence-diverse protein families currently annotated with unknown or generic functions, aiming to solve representative structures across their phylogenetic breadth. By clustering these structures by fold and active site geometry—rather than sequence similarity—the approach can identify divergent members that have evolved to perform specialized functions, such as resolving distinct DNA junction topologies or processing novel substrates. This structural phylogenomics framework directly links evolutionary divergence in fold to the emergence of new biochemical activities, systematically converting 'unknowns' into mechanistically understood functional subfamilies.

ideas.title: Structural Metagenomics of Metazoan Genomes to Uncover Novel HJ Resolution Folds
ideas.description: This project proposes a structural metagenomics approach focused on metazoan systems to discover entirely novel Holliday junction resolvase folds. We will computationally predict and experimentally determine high-throughput protein structures from diverse animal genomes and transcriptomes, screening specifically for proteins that possess structural features indicative of four-way DNA junction binding and cleavage, such as symmetric dimers or tetramers with catalytic residues positioned for dual-strand incision. This direct structure-to-function pipeline is designed to identify non-homologous enzymes that perform HJ resolution using novel chemistry, a class that would be invisible to any sequence-based search. The outcome would be the first systematic catalog of metazoan HJ resolution machinery defined by its structural, rather than evolutionary, relationship.

ideas.title: Structural genetic array mapping of divergent DNA repair complexes
ideas.description: This project integrates synthetic genetic array (SGA) analysis with high-throughput structural genomics to systematically dissect the genetic interaction networks of proteins based on their structural folds, rather than sequence homology. We will first use structural determination pipelines to identify divergent, non-homologous HJ resolvase candidates in yeast by their conserved structural features. These structurally identified proteins will then be used as query genes in large-scale SGA screens to map their comprehensive genetic interaction landscapes. This combined approach will reveal how specific structural scaffolds in the TRF and resolvase families create unique, fold-specific genetic dependencies, uncovering novel functional relationships in genome maintenance that are invisible to sequence-based methods alone.

ideas.title: Viral RuvC resolvases as inducible tools for cell-specific telomere uncapping and DNA damage signaling
ideas.description: This project first expands the bioinformatic search for RuvC-like resolvases to diverse viral families, prioritizing candidates with high activity and potential for minimal off-target effects in metazoan genomes. Selected viral resolvases are then fused to a telomere-targeting domain (e.g., derived from TRF1 or TRF2) and placed under the control of tissue-specific or inducible promoters in model organisms. This system allows for the precise, acute ablation of telomere caps in defined cell types or developmental stages by cleaving the telomeric DNA itself, directly testing the protective function of TRF family members and triggering localized DNA damage responses. The approach bypasses the slow, systemic phenotypes of conventional knockouts to reveal the immediate consequences of telomere dysfunction in specific developmental contexts.

ideas.title: Expand superfamily classification using multi-scale, context-aware clustering
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to update protein superfamily classification by integrating new sequence data, analogous to how astronomy expanded galaxy classification using multi-wavelength data. In astronomy, integrating radio, infrared, optical, and X-ray observations with environmental context revealed hidden relationships and transitional objects. Adapting this solution, we will develop a multi-scale, context-aware clustering method for protein sequences. This method will integrate orthogonal 'wavelengths' of data—including predicted structural features, conserved functional motifs, intrinsic disorder profiles, and genomic neighborhood context—alongside primary sequence. By doing so, it will identify highly divergent superfamily members that are missed by standard sequence-similarity searches, thereby creating a more comprehensive, evolutionarily informed, and functionally predictive classification system as genomic databases continue to grow.

ideas.title: Adapting geological prospecting methods to identify divergent resolvases
ideas.description: This idea adapts the methodology of mineral exploration geophysics to the problem of identifying divergent HJ resolvases. In geophysics, broad surveys detect subsurface structural anomalies that signal potential mineral deposits, independent of surface chemistry. Similarly, we propose a computational structural genomics 'prospecting' pipeline. Using large-scale protein structure prediction databases (e.g., AlphaFold2 proteome sets) and sensitive structural comparison tools (like Foldseek or Dali), we systematically scan for proteins that possess the core structural hallmarks of resolvases (e.g., a conserved RNase H-like fold with key catalytic residue geometry) but lack significant sequence similarity to known families. This structure-first survey identifies 'structural anomalies'—proteins with the correct fold in unexpected genomic contexts or taxa. High-confidence structural hits are then prioritized for experimental validation of Holliday junction resolution activity, enabling discovery based purely on conserved structural logic.

ideas.title: Challenge that all kinase-like families are true kinases
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from the field of Protein Kinase Biology, where the discovery and characterization of 'pseudokinases' challenged the assumption that all proteins with a kinase fold are active enzymes. The analogous solution involved systematic biochemical assays for phosphotransfer activity, coupled with structural studies to confirm the loss of catalytic machinery and elucidate alternative functions like allosteric regulation or scaffolding. Adapted to this research context, the idea is to biochemically test predicted nucleotidyltransferase families for a loss of catalytic activity by assaying for nucleotidyl transfer and checking for degenerate active-site motifs. The subsequent phase would involve using structural biology and interaction studies to determine if these inactive families have gained novel regulatory roles, such as acting as competitive inhibitors, molecular decoys, or allosteric modulators within pathways involving canonical nucleotidyltransferases.

ideas.title: Employ deep mutational scanning on minimal domain using high-throughput screening principles from materials science
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from high-throughput combinatorial screening in materials science to the problem of mapping functional constraints on a proposed ancestral protein domain via deep mutational scanning. In materials science, combinatorial libraries of material compositions are created on a single substrate and screened in parallel with automated probes to map property landscapes. Adapted to this biological context, we propose synthesizing a comprehensive, spatially-addressable library of all single (and possibly double) mutants of the minimal domain directly on a high-density DNA array surface. This mutant 'chip' would then be used in a massively parallel in vitro transcription/translation and binding assay. A high-content, automated readout (e.g., fluorescence from binding a tagged target) would measure functional fitness for each mutant position simultaneously. This approach would generate a complete fitness landscape for the ancestral domain, identifying critical residues, functional epitopes, and permissible substitutions, thereby revealing the domain's fundamental design principles and evolutionary constraints.

ideas.title: Pan-genomic target prioritization for broad-spectrum inhibitors of bacterial signaling nucleotidyltransferases
ideas.description: This research direction integrates large-scale pan-genomic analysis with structure-based drug design to develop novel antibiotics. First, we will analyze the distribution, phyletic patterns, and horizontal gene transfer events of bacterial signaling nucleotidyltransferases across thousands of bacterial genomes. This map will identify conserved, core family members essential across pathogens as well as recently transferred, niche-specific variants. Using this evolutionary and genomic framework, we will then prioritize specific enzyme targets for inhibitor design that offer the optimal combination of broad-spectrum activity and reduced likelihood of pre-existing resistance. Finally, computational and experimental methods will be used to design small molecules that selectively inhibit these prioritized, high-value targets within the superfamily.

ideas.title: Adapting Antibody-Antigen Discovery for Signaling Domain Ligand Identification
ideas.description: This idea adapts the established high-throughput screening method from therapeutic antibody discovery—where phage display libraries of antibody fragments are panned against target antigens—to the problem of finding ligands for signaling protein binding domains. In the analogous field, this approach successfully identifies high-affinity, specific binders for therapeutic targets. The adapted solution involves creating or using existing phage display libraries (e.g., random peptide, cDNA, or structured domain libraries) and performing biopanning experiments against an immobilized, purified binding domain from a signaling protein family. The output will be a set of identified peptide or protein sequences that bind the domain, which can then be validated and used to map novel protein-protein interactions within cellular signaling networks, potentially revealing new regulatory mechanisms or drug targets.

ideas.title: Cross-species genetic interaction mapping of TRF family networks integrating yeast SGA and mammalian CRISPR screens
ideas.description: This redesigned project employs a dual-platform strategy to map the genetic interaction networks of TRF family proteins with high confidence in their biological relevance. It first uses yeast synthetic genetic array (SGA) analysis to generate a comprehensive, unbiased interaction map in a tractable model system. In parallel, a focused CRISPR-Cas9-based combinatorial knockout screen is conducted in mammalian cells targeting conserved TRF orthologs and a curated set of candidate interactors derived from proteomic and bioinformatic data. By systematically comparing the interaction networks from both systems, the approach distinguishes evolutionarily conserved, functionally core genetic interactions from species-specific or context-dependent ones, resulting in a robust and validated genetic network map for the TRF family.

ideas.title: Integrated multi-method structural genomics for uncharacterized protein families
ideas.description: This adversarially improved approach establishes a multi-tiered structural determination pipeline to overcome the crystallization bottleneck. It first uses computational predictions to prioritize targets and select the most suitable experimental method on a case-by-case basis. The core system integrates high-throughput crystallography for amenable targets, single-particle cryo-EM for large complexes and membrane proteins, and NMR for dynamic or disordered regions. This integrated, method-agnostic strategy ensures a robust and higher-yield determination of novel family structures regardless of their biophysical properties.

ideas.title: Functional Complementation Screen for Metazoan Holliday Junction Resolution Activity
ideas.description: This redesigned project abandons a pure sequence-homology search to instead directly screen for the biochemical function of HJ resolution. A high-throughput functional complementation system is established in yeast, where a resolvase-deficient strain, carrying a fluorescent reporter for successful HJ resolution, is used to screen expression libraries from key metazoan models (e.g., C. elegans, D. melanogaster, human). Hits that rescue the resolution defect are isolated and their gene products rigorously characterized using in vitro HJ cleavage assays and structural biology. This approach is agnostic to protein family and is designed to identify novel, metazoan-specific HJ resolvases or moonlighting activities from known proteins.

ideas.title: Inverse Problem: Design Nucleotide Analogs to Probe and Inhibit Eukaryotic Chromatin-Modifying Nucleotidyltransferases
ideas.description: This research direction applies the inhibitor-design framework from bacterial signaling nucleotidyltransferases to the uncharacterized nucleotide polymerization reactions of eukaryotic TRF family enzymes. The core strategy is to synthesize rationally designed nucleotide analogs and transition-state mimics based on hypothesized TRF substrates; these molecules will serve dual purposes as chemical tools to experimentally determine the enzymes' true substrates and products (characterization) and as potential inhibitors to assess their viability as drug targets. This creates a closed-loop discovery engine where biochemical characterization directly informs inhibitor optimization, and inhibitor binding studies validate mechanistic hypotheses. The ultimate goal is to identify first-in-class chemical probes targeting chromatin-modifying nucleotidyltransferases, opening new avenues for epigenetic therapy.

ideas.title: Compartment-Specific Validation of Non-Chromatin RNA Tailing by TRF Family Enzymes
ideas.description: This project directly tests the hypothesis that TRF family enzymes can modify RNA in the cytoplasm, moving beyond their established nuclear chromatin roles. To overcome the critical flaw of subcellular contamination, the research employs rigorous biochemical fractionation coupled with orthogonal, inducible relocalization assays to forcibly mislocalize TRFs and observe consequent RNA modification. It integrates these approaches with live-cell biosensors to detect RNA tailing events in situ within the cytoplasm. This multi-layered validation framework ensures any observed activity is a genuine non-nuclear function, not an artifact.

ideas.title: Applying mitochondrial protein moonlighting paradigm to TRF enzymes
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that TRF family enzymes function only in chromatin by adapting a solution from mitochondrial biology. In that field, numerous proteins with well-characterized nuclear roles (e.g., in DNA repair or transcription) were later found to have independent, essential functions in mitochondria, a discovery driven by techniques like systematic localization, organelle-specific biochemical assays, and analysis of targeting signals. The adapted research idea applies this paradigm: first, use high-resolution imaging and subcellular fractionation to systematically test for TRF presence in the cytoplasm. Second, perform functional biochemical assays (e.g., for non-polyadenylation RNA tailing) on purified cytoplasmic fractions. Third, bioinformatically and experimentally search for TRF isoforms, sequence motifs, or post-translational modifications that could direct cytoplasmic localization, analogous to mitochondrial targeting sequences.

ideas.title: Adapting orphan receptor deorphanization strategies to characterize novel bacterial nucleotidyltransferase signaling systems
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to adapt the established 'orphan receptor deorphanization' strategies from eukaryotic GPCR pharmacology to solve the analogous problem of identifying ligands and pathways for a newly discovered family of bacterial/archaeal signal transduction nucleotidyltransferases. In GPCR research, orphan receptors are characterized by expressing them in engineered cell lines and screening compound libraries while monitoring second messenger activation. Adapted to this microbial context, the nucleotidyltransferases will be heterologously expressed in a genetically tractable bacterial host (e.g., E. coli) engineered with transcriptional reporters or biosensors sensitive to potential nucleotide signaling molecules. The system will then be used in a reverse pharmacology approach: first, to screen purified candidate ligands (nucleotides, nucleotide derivatives) for enzyme activation; and second, as an assay platform to fractionate and identify activating molecules from the native organism's metabolome. This will systematically identify the natural ligands (the 'input signal') and characterize the immediate enzymatic product (the 'output signal'), thereby defining the core transduction pathway for this novel protein family.

ideas.title: Polypharmacological Inhibitor Design Against Essential Bacterial Signaling Nucleotidyltransferases
ideas.description: This project aims to develop a novel class of antibiotics with a high barrier to resistance by designing a single small molecule that inhibits multiple essential bacterial signaling nucleotidyltransferases. Using advanced computational methods like molecular docking, pharmacophore modeling, and machine learning on structural databases, the research will identify or design compounds with polypharmacological profiles against key, non-homologous human enzyme targets. The lead molecules will be validated for simultaneous binding and inhibition of the selected bacterial enzyme targets, and their efficacy and resistance propensity will be tested against pathogenic bacteria in vitro and in infection models.

ideas.description: This redesigned study first defines the in vivo signaling context for the nucleotidyltransferase family by implementing a high-throughput genetic screen that couples promoter activity to dual fluorescent reporters in Escherichia coli or Bacillus subtilis. This screen identifies the specific environmental stimuli or cellular states that activate the pathway. Subsequently, using the physiologically relevant condition as a guide, ligand identification is performed via targeted affinity capture from native cell lysates followed by metabolomic analysis and validation using isothermal titration calorimetry and X-ray crystallography of the ligand-binding domain. This function-first approach ensures that any identified ligand is directly linked to a validated signaling role and biological output.

ideas.title: Force-FRET: Correlating Conformational Dynamics and Mechanical Output in Minimal Polymerases
ideas.description: This study integrates single-molecule FRET with magnetic tweezers force spectroscopy to dissect the mechano-chemical coupling in minimal archaeal polymerases. By applying controlled mechanical tension to a DNA template during nucleotide addition, the system can stabilize transient functional conformations and directly correlate FRET-reported structural dynamics with the physical work output (e.g., DNA translocation or template scrunching). This dual-measurement approach overcomes the ambiguity of studying ultra-dynamic minimal proteins by ensuring observed FRET transitions are linked to the enzyme's catalytic cycle and mechanical action. The result is a robust, multi-parameter characterization of how minimal folds achieve processive catalysis under mechanical load.
