ideas.title: Use clickable metabolic labeling and affinity purification to isolate novel TRF-catalyzed nucleotide polymers
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from glycobiology, where metabolic oligosaccharide engineering (MOE) uses clickable sugar analogs to tag, capture, and identify novel glycans. The analogous problem of finding rare products of specific enzymes is solved by incorporating bioorthogonal chemical handles via metabolism for selective downstream isolation. Adapted to nucleotide research, cells will be fed with clickable nucleotide analogs (e.g., 5-ethynyluridine) that can serve as substrates for TRF family enzymes. The novel polymers synthesized by these enzymes will then be covalently tagged via click chemistry with a biotin probe, allowing for robust streptavidin-based affinity purification. This method isolates the novel TRF products from the background of canonical nucleic acids, enabling their characterization by sequencing or mass spectrometry to reveal their composition and structure.

ideas.title: Scale down and track out: Acute, pan-TRF depletion with resolution of cell-autonomous and non-autonomous effects
ideas.description: This redesigned system addresses the limitations of traditional knockouts by employing acute, inducible degradation of TRF proteins in specific cell types using degron technology, eliminating issues of protein perdurance. To overcome compensation by related family members, the approach uses a pre-existing genetic background where all TRF genes are floxed, allowing for simultaneous, Cre-mediated knockout of the entire family upon degradation initiation. The response is then monitored with high temporal resolution using single-cell multi-omics, enabling the dissection of cell-autonomous transcriptional changes from secondary, non-cell-autonomous effects on the surrounding tissue microenvironment during development.

ideas.title: Circuit-Specific Knockdown: Adapting Network Node Isolation from Neuroscience to Telomere Biology
ideas.description: The original idea of using tissue-specific knockouts to dissect TRF function is analogous to the challenge in systems neuroscience of determining the role of a specific signaling molecule within a complex, interconnected network. Neuroscience solved this by moving beyond regional lesions to techniques like chemogenetics (DREADDs), which allow for the reversible and cell-type-specific inhibition of neuronal activity within defined circuits. Adapting this solution, the research idea is to implement a 'circuit-specific knockdown' for telomere biology. By engineering a degron-tagged allele of a TRF family member (e.g., TRF2) and crossing it with a driver expressing the degradation machinery (e.g., plant TIR1) in a highly specific cell lineage, we can achieve acute, inducible, and spatially restricted protein depletion. This allows us to dissect TRF function with much finer granularity—targeting, for example, only hematopoietic stem cells within the bone marrow or a specific neuronal progenitor population in the brain—thereby isolating its role in a defined 'cellular circuit' during metazoan development without confounding systemic or compensatory effects.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporal mapping of TRF-catalyzed non-canonical nucleotide polymers in chromatin using pulse-chase metabolic labeling
ideas.description: This fused approach combines targeted metabolic labeling with biochemical characterization to dynamically track the synthesis and fate of TRF-catalyzed nucleotide polymers in living cells. By applying pulse-chase strategies with radiolabeled or chemically tagged nucleotide precursors, we can identify the specific substrates incorporated by individual TRF family members and isolate their novel polymeric products directly from chromatin fractions. This enables the correlation of polymerization events with specific chromatin states, cell cycle phases, or DNA damage responses, revealing the functional context of these reactions. Consequently, the method moves beyond mere detection to establish the enzymatic specificity, genomic localization, and turnover kinetics of previously unknown nucleotide polymers involved in chromatin maintenance.
